name: The Counting Lesson
text:
Your uncle teaches you to count money by making you steal it.

Not from strangers. From him. He puts coins in his coat — different pockets, different amounts — and walks through the market. You follow. Your job is to take as many as you can without him feeling it.

The first week, you get nothing. His hands catch yours every time. He doesn't hit you. He just looks at you with the particular disappointment of a man who expected better from someone he's investing in.

The second week, you get two coppers from the outside pocket. He buys you dinner with them.

The third week, he starts leaving the coins in harder places. Inside lining. Boot cuff. The band of his hat. You learn that fingers work better cold — warm fingers stick to fabric. You learn that crowds help because people bump each other and nobody tracks which bump was on purpose. You learn that the best moment to reach is when your uncle is talking to someone, because people stop feeling their body when their mouth is working.

By the fourth month you can empty every pocket he has during a short walk. He stops the exercise. Not because you've mastered it. Because you emptied the pockets of the man he was talking to as well, and neither of them noticed until the man got home.

Your uncle doesn't praise you. He goes quiet for a while. Then he says, 'Don't do that to people who haven't earned it.' He doesn't define who's earned it. You spend years figuring out your own answer.
